nodes.title: The Thera (Santorini) eruption dates to c. 1600 BCE (high chronology)
nodes.body:
The **radiocarbon-driven** date for the Bronze Age eruption that buried
[[site-akrotiri-thera]]: roughly the late 17th to mid-16th century BCE. The
foundational exhibit is the [[source-friedrich-2006|buried olive branch]]
wiggle-matched to 1627–1600 BCE; later annual-resolution ¹⁴C work
([[source-pearson-2018]]) supports a 16th-century date and narrows the offset.

**Contested against** [[claim-thera-low-chronology]], the archaeologically-derived
date about a century later. The gap matters: it re-times the entire east
Mediterranean Late Bronze Age and the Aegean–Egyptian synchronisms. Held at 0.6 —
the scientific dating has strengthened the high chronology, but the conflict with
Egyptian historical chronology is real and unresolved, so this is not called
settled.

nodes.title: The Thera eruption dates to c. 1500 BCE (low / archaeological chronology)
nodes.body:
The traditional, **archaeologically-derived** date: the eruption falls after the
start of the Egyptian New Kingdom, around 1500 BCE (or a little earlier). It rests
on synchronisms between Aegean, Levantine, and Egyptian material culture —
pottery styles and trade goods found above and below the eruption layers.

It [[claim-thera-high-chronology|disputes the ~1600 BCE radiocarbon date]] by
about a century. Proponents argue the ¹⁴C results are distorted (e.g. by
volcanic CO₂ or calibration-curve plateaus) and that the cross-cultural
archaeology should anchor the date. Held at 0.4: it remains a serious position
defended by respected Aegean archaeologists, but the weight of recent scientific
dating has shifted against it — a genuinely live minority view rather than a
refuted one.

nodes.title: The Tomb I male skeleton shows knee ankylosis matching Philip's lameness
nodes.body:
The physical evidence underpinning [[claim-tomb-i-philip-ii]]. The male skeleton
in [[site-tomb-i]] displays a severe **knee ankylosis** — the joint fused at
roughly 79° of flexion — pierced by a hole that [[source-bartsiokas-2015]] reads
as a healed penetrating wound. This is matched to the historically attested lance
wound that left Philip II permanently lame in 339 BC.

The inference chain is: pathology → specific historical wound → individual
identity. Each link is contestable — knee ankylosis has other causes, and tying
one lesion to one recorded wound is an argument by resemblance, not proof. But it
is a genuine, striking correspondence and the paper's central exhibit. Confidence
0.5: the observation is real and well documented; the identification it is
marshalled for is not settled.

nodes.title: Vergina Tomb I holds Philip II (the lameness argument)
nodes.body:
A third position, advanced by [[scholar-antonis-bartsiokas]] in
[[source-bartsiokas-2015]]: Philip II is not in Tomb II at all, but in the
adjacent, earlier, looted [[site-tomb-i]]. The case is the
[[claim-tomb-i-knee-lameness|knee-ankylosis]] of the Tomb I male, matched to the
historically documented spear wound that left Philip lame in 339 BC.

If accepted, this reassigns [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]] to Arrhidaios (agreeing
with [[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]] on Tomb II while disagreeing about where
Philip II lies). It is the minority view: it depends on associating a specific
pathology with one historical wound, and on dating Tomb I's occupants to
Philip's family. Held at 0.4 — a serious, published hypothesis that has not won
consensus.

nodes.title: Vergina Tomb II holds Philip III Arrhidaios (not Philip II)
nodes.body:
The principal rival to [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]]: the main-chamber male of
[[site-tomb-ii]] is **Philip III Arrhidaios**, Alexander's half-brother and
short-lived successor (died 317 BC), buried with his wife Eurydice. This dates
the tomb roughly two decades later than Philip II's death.

The argument rests on two legs. First, chronology of the grave goods
(some scholars read the pottery and artefacts as post-320 BC). Second, the
forensic claim in [[source-bartsiokas-2000]] that the bones were cremated **dry**
— degreased and unfleshed — implying a body exhumed and burned long after death,
consistent with Arrhidaios's disturbed reburial rather than a fresh royal pyre.

That second leg is exactly what [[claim-tomb-ii-green-cremation]] and
[[source-musgrave-2010]] attack: they argue the bones were burned fleshed, which
undercuts the Arrhidaios reconstruction. Held at 0.45 — the mirror of its rival,
because the debate is unresolved.

nodes.title: The Tomb II male was cremated 'green' (fleshed), not dry
nodes.body:
The forensic crux of the whole Vergina debate. **Green cremation** = the body was
burned soon after death with flesh still on the bone; **dry cremation** = a
skeleton burned long after decomposition. The two leave different signatures in
bone colour and in the pattern of warping and fracture lines.

[[source-bartsiokas-2000]] argued for **dry** cremation, supporting
[[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]] (a delayed, disturbed reburial).
[[source-musgrave-2010]] argued for **green** cremation — transverse fracture
patterns and colouration consistent with a fleshed body — which supports a fresh
royal pyre and therefore [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]].

This note is the single point where the physical evidence most directly decides
between the two Tomb II identifications, which is why both camps invest so heavily
in it. Confidence 0.6: the green reading is somewhat better supported in the
recent literature, but the dry reading is not dead.

nodes.title: Vergina Tomb II holds Philip II of Macedon
nodes.body:
The original and still most widely held identification: the cremated male in the
main chamber of [[site-tomb-ii]] is **Philip II of Macedon**, father of Alexander
the Great, assassinated in 336 BC. Proposed by the excavator
[[scholar-manolis-andronikos]] in 1977–78 on the basis of the tomb's royal
richness, its date, and later the [[claim-tomb-ii-green-cremation|green-cremation]]
and facial-asymmetry evidence assembled by [[scholar-john-prag]] and
[[scholar-jonathan-musgrave]].

**Status: genuinely contested.** The rival reading —
[[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]] — reads the same bones as Philip III Arrhidaios.
The system does **not** pick a side: both identifications have peer-reviewed
forensic support and neither has produced a decisive artifact (no inscription
names the occupant). Confidence is held near 0.5 to reflect a live scholarly
split, not ignorance.

Evidence for is marshalled most fully in [[source-musgrave-2010]]. The strongest
dissent is [[source-bartsiokas-2000]] and, for a different tomb entirely,
[[source-bartsiokas-2015]].

nodes.title: Antonis Bartsiokas
nodes.body:
Greek palaeoanthropologist (Democritus University of Thrace) and the most
persistent challenger of Andronikos's identification. Author of
[[source-bartsiokas-2000]] (Tomb II bones are Arrhidaios, cremated dry) and lead
author of [[source-bartsiokas-2015]] (Philip II is actually in
[[site-tomb-i]]).

His two papers anchor the two dissenting claims —
[[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]] and [[claim-tomb-i-philip-ii]] — and between them
they force the field to treat the identification as an open forensic question
rather than settled fact.

nodes.title: A. J. N. W. (John) Prag
nodes.body:
Classicist and curator (Manchester Museum) who, with Richard Neave, created the
1980s facial reconstruction of the Tomb II skull and read its asymmetry as
evidence of Philip II's eye wound. Co-author of [[source-musgrave-2010]].

His reconstruction is the positive case that [[source-bartsiokas-2000]] set out
to dismantle — the orbital "scar" Prag read as a healed wound, Bartsiokas read as
a cremation artefact. Prag is therefore a hub where the [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]]
argument and its rebuttal meet.

nodes.title: Jonathan Musgrave (with J. Prag & R. Neave)
nodes.body:
Anatomist at the University of Bristol and, with John Prag and the medical
artist Richard Neave, part of the team that produced the famous facial
reconstruction of the Tomb II skull and defended the Philip II identification.
Lead author of [[source-musgrave-2010]].

Grouped here as one node with the [[scholar-john-prag|Prag]]–Neave collaboration
because they argue as a unit. They are the standard-bearers for
[[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]] and the [[claim-tomb-ii-green-cremation|green
cremation]] reading — and, having built the original reconstruction, they are
defending their own earlier conclusion, which the graph records rather than
hides.

nodes.title: Manolis Andronikos (1919–1992)
nodes.body:
Greek archaeologist who excavated the [[site-great-tumulus]] at
[[site-aigai-vergina]] in 1977–78 and announced the unlooted royal tombs to the
world. He identified [[site-tomb-ii]] as the burial of **Philip II** — the
founding claim, [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]] — and staked his professional legacy
on it.

His identification is the historical root of the entire debate: every later
paper, whether it defends or attacks Philip II, is arguing with Andronikos.
Tracking him as a node makes the graph show *why* the literature clusters the way
it does — the dispute is, in part, about whether the excavator got it right.

nodes.title: Sturt W. Manning
nodes.body:
Archaeologist and dendrochronologist (Cornell University), a leading voice for the
radiocarbon-based [[claim-thera-high-chronology|high chronology]] of the Thera
eruption and a prolific writer on the Aegean–Egyptian dating conflict. Co-author
of [[source-pearson-2018]].

Included as the Thera cluster's scholar node — the counterpart to
[[scholar-antonis-bartsiokas]] in the Vergina cluster — so the graph shows that
each debate has identifiable protagonists, not just disembodied "evidence."

nodes.title: Aigai (modern Vergina), Macedonia
nodes.body:
The ancient first capital of the kingdom of Macedon, at modern Vergina in northern
Greece — a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Its identification as Aigai (rather than
Edessa, an older guess) is now settled, reinforced by the royal necropolis and
palace.

Aigai is the geographic anchor of the Vergina cluster. It contains the
[[site-great-tumulus]], which in turn contains [[site-tomb-i]] and
[[site-tomb-ii]]. As a *site* node it is a place, not a document — no source
citation is attached, by design: the anti-hallucination rule governs source
nodes, and places are established context.

nodes.title: Akrotiri, Thera (Santorini)
nodes.body:
The Late Bronze Age Cycladic town on the island of Thera (Santorini), buried and
preserved under metres of volcanic tephra by the eruption — the "Aegean Pompeii,"
with multi-storey houses and wall frescoes.

Akrotiri is the geographic anchor of the Thera cluster. The eruption that sealed
it is what both [[claim-thera-high-chronology]] and [[claim-thera-low-chronology]]
are trying to date. A place node, not a document.

nodes.title: The Great Tumulus, Vergina
nodes.body:
The great earthen mound at [[site-aigai-vergina]] raised over the royal tombs,
excavated by [[scholar-manolis-andronikos]] in 1977–78. It covers at least three
tombs: the looted [[site-tomb-i]], the unlooted [[site-tomb-ii]], and Tomb III
(the "Prince's Tomb").

The tumulus is the container that ties the whole Vergina dispute together — the
argument is really about *which body in which chamber under this mound* is which
Macedonian royal. It is the natural hub node of the cluster.

nodes.title: Tomb I (the looted 'Tomb of Persephone'), Vergina
nodes.body:
The earlier, cist-type tomb under the [[site-great-tumulus]], looted in antiquity
but famous for its Rape of Persephone wall painting. It contained the disturbed
remains of a man, a woman, and a newborn.

Long treated as a secondary tomb, it moved to centre stage with
[[source-bartsiokas-2015]], which argues its male skeleton — with a fused,
pierced knee ([[claim-tomb-i-knee-lameness]]) — is the true Philip II
([[claim-tomb-i-philip-ii]]). Whether that reassignment holds is unresolved.

nodes.title: Tomb II (the unlooted 'Tomb of Philip'), Vergina
nodes.body:
The unlooted, two-chambered barrel-vaulted tomb under the [[site-great-tumulus]],
found intact in 1977 with a wealth of royal armour, gold, and two cremation
burials (a man in the main chamber, a woman in the antechamber).

Its main-chamber male is the single most fought-over skeleton in Greek
archaeology: claimed for Philip II ([[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]]) and for Philip
III Arrhidaios ([[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]]). Because the tomb bears no
inscription naming its occupant, the identification rests entirely on
[[claim-tomb-ii-green-cremation|forensic]] and chronological argument — which is
exactly why it stays contested.

nodes.title: Bartsiokas 2000 — The Eye Injury of King Philip II and the Skeletal Evidence from Royal Tomb II at Vergina
nodes.body:
**Peer-reviewed article, Science, 2000.** Verified via search of the Science
record and the surrounding literature (the paper is widely cited by both camps).

Re-examines the cremated Tomb II skull. Argues that the orbital scar earlier read
as [[scholar-john-prag|Prag]]'s evidence of Philip II's famous eye wound is in
fact an artefact of cremation, and that the post-cranial skeleton lacks the
trauma Philip's documented injuries would predict. Concludes the bones were burned
**dry** (degreased, unfleshed) and belong to [[scholar-antonis-bartsiokas]]'s
proposed occupant, Philip III Arrhidaios — i.e. it supports
[[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]] and attacks [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]].

This is a **primary node in the dispute**: directly answered by
[[source-musgrave-2010]]. In graph terms it is one root of the Tomb II
citation funnel — a large share of the "Arrhidaios" argument traces back here.

nodes.title: Bartsiokas et al. 2015 — The lameness of King Philip II and Royal Tomb I at Vergina
nodes.body:
**Peer-reviewed article, PNAS, 2015.** Fetched and verified via PubMed Central
(PMC4538655); full citation and DOI confirmed from the article record.

Shifts [[scholar-antonis-bartsiokas]]'s argument to a different tomb. Reports a
knee ankylosis (fused at ~79°, pierced by a healed penetrating lesion) in the
[[site-tomb-i]] male — see [[claim-tomb-i-knee-lameness]] — and reads it as the
lameness Philip II suffered from a 339 BC lance wound. Concludes Philip II lies in
Tomb I, with Cleopatra and their newborn; and, consequently, that
[[site-tomb-ii]] holds Arrhidaios and Eurydice.

Supports [[claim-tomb-i-philip-ii]]; disputes [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]]. A
striking, much-discussed paper that nonetheless remains a minority position —
covered in the synthesis [[source-vergina-review-2023]].

nodes.title: Ehrlich et al. 2023 — Olive shrub buried on Therasia supports a mid-16th century BCE date for the Thera eruption
nodes.body:
**Peer-reviewed article, Scientific Reports, 2023 (open access).** Verified via
the Nature record and PMC (PMC10147620); the fetch hit an IdP redirect but
citation and result are confirmed from the indexed record and abstract.

Reports a second, independent buried olive — a shrub carbonised in life position
on neighbouring Therasia — radiocarbon-dated to the **mid-16th century BCE**,
slightly younger than the [[source-friedrich-2006|1627–1600 BCE branch]]. The
authors read it as support for a high (16th-c) date while noting the persistent
gap with the archaeological chronology.

A useful stress-test node: a *new primary datum* rather than a review, it nudges
[[claim-thera-high-chronology]] later within the high-chronology range and
tightens the case against [[claim-thera-low-chronology]] — showing the graph can
represent evidence that supports one claim while only partly agreeing with an
allied source.

nodes.title: Friedrich et al. 2006 — Santorini Eruption Radiocarbon Dated to 1627–1600 B.C.
nodes.body:
**Peer-reviewed article, Science, 2006.** Fetched and verified via the PubMed
record (PMID 16645088); author list and result confirmed.

Reports an olive tree buried alive in growth position by the Thera tephra.
Radiocarbon wiggle-matching of successive tree-ring segments constrains the
eruption to **1627–1600 BCE at 95.4% probability** — about a century earlier than
the Egyptian-synchronism date. The keystone exhibit for
[[claim-thera-high-chronology]].

A root of the high-chronology citation funnel: much later argument leans on this
single buried branch, which is also why the branch itself became contested (its
ring count and the assumption it died in the eruption have both been questioned).
A clean example of why the graph distinguishes a primary datum from the many
papers that echo it.

nodes.title: Musgrave et al. 2010 — The Occupants of Tomb II at Vergina: Why Arrhidaios and Eurydice must be excluded
nodes.body:
**Peer-reviewed article, International Journal of Medical Sciences, 2010
(open access).** Verified via the journal record and Bristol's research
repository.

A point-by-point rebuttal of [[source-bartsiokas-2000]]. Its central forensic
claim: the colour and transverse fracture lines of the Tomb II bones indicate a
**green** (fleshed) cremation, not a dry one — see
[[claim-tomb-ii-green-cremation]]. The authors argue this weakens rather than
strengthens the Arrhidaios case, and that the skull's facial asymmetry is
consistent with Philip II's eye wound. Supports [[claim-tomb-ii-philip-ii]];
disputes [[claim-tomb-ii-arrhidaios]].

Authored by the [[scholar-john-prag|Prag]]–Neave–[[scholar-jonathan-musgrave|Musgrave]]
team behind the original facial reconstruction, so it is **evidence *and*
interested party** — a good example of why the graph tracks who argues what, not
just the conclusion.

nodes.title: Pearson et al. 2018 — Annual radiocarbon record indicates 16th-century BCE date for the Thera eruption
nodes.body:
**Peer-reviewed article, Science Advances, 2018 (open access).** Verified via the
journal record and PMC (PMC6093623); the paywalled HTML blocked a direct read but
the citation, authorship, and result are confirmed across the indexed record.

Uses annual-resolution radiocarbon measurements on known-age tree rings to refine
the calibration curve around the eruption window. Concludes the data indicate a
**16th-century BCE** date, narrowing — though not eliminating — the offset from
the archaeological chronology. Supports [[claim-thera-high-chronology]];
co-authored by [[scholar-sturt-manning]].

Sits *downstream* of [[source-friedrich-2006]] in the argument but is
methodologically independent (calibration, not the buried branch), so it
genuinely strengthens the high chronology rather than merely echoing it — the
distinction the citogenesis check is built to surface.

nodes.title: Maniatis et al. 2023 — The identification of the Royal Tombs in the Great Tumulus at Vergina: A comprehensive review
nodes.body:
**Secondary / review article, 2023.** Verified via the ScienceDirect record;
listed here as a *review* (echo), not primary evidence — an important distinction
this project makes explicit.

A comprehensive survey of the Vergina identification debate, weighing the
radiocarbon, osteological, and archaeological arguments across all three Great
Tumulus tombs. It **cites and summarises** the primary forensic work rather than
generating new skeletal evidence, so in the graph it points *up* at the claims
and at [[site-great-tumulus]] as a synthesis node.

Included deliberately as a citogenesis check: a reader who found only this review
might treat "the evidence" as one coherent body, when it is in fact a contested
pile of [[source-bartsiokas-2000]], [[source-musgrave-2010]], and
[[source-bartsiokas-2015]] pulling in different directions.
